meta.title: Consumer GPU VRAM Price Reversion by 2028
meta.question: By 2028-12-31, has the street price of top-tier consumer discrete GPUs (RTX 5080-class or nearest equivalent-tier) reverted to within 20% of the inflation-adjusted equivalent-tier consumer GPU price from 2024-Q4, as tracked by major GPU pricing databases?
meta.resolution: Resolved H1 (full reversion) if NowInStock, TechPowerUp, or equivalent shows RTX 5080-class 90-day average street price within 20% of inflation-adjusted 2024-Q4 RTX 4080 baseline (est. $1,050-$1,150 CPI-adjusted) for ≥3 consecutive months before 2028-12-31. H2 if 20-50% above baseline at resolution date. H3 if 50-100% above. H4 if >100% above baseline or NVIDIA exits consumer discrete GPU segment. resolutionDate: 2028-12-31.

tagConfig._docs: SUPPLY/HARDWARE favor H1/H2 (normalization). DEMAND/EARNINGS (hyperscaler) favor H3/H4. REGULATORY neutral.

H1.label: Full reversion: street price within 20% of inflation-adj pre-surge (Q4-24) baseline
H1.description: Malone's 'short-term market problem' thesis — new fab capacity (TSMC 2nm, HBM4), AMD competition, and inference efficiency gains normalize consumer GPU supply/demand. RTX 5080-class returns to historical tier pricing by 2028.

H2.label: Moderate elevated plateau: 20-50% above inflation-adj pre-surge baseline
H2.description: Data-center demand partially eases but doesn't fully normalize. New fab capacity reduces scarcity but doesn't eliminate it. Pricing settles above historical norms but consumers can still buy at some premium.

H3.label: Sustained high elevation: 50-100% above inflation-adj pre-surge baseline
H3.description: Alex's thesis — AI/data-center demand structurally outpaces consumer GPU supply. HBM allocation remains prioritized for enterprise. Consumers face persistent scarcity and elevated prices through 2028. lowrhoufo '10-year horizon' view plausible.

H4.label: Structural escalation/bifurcation: street price over 100% above pre-surge baseline or NVIDIA exits consumer GPU
H4.description: NVIDIA formally de-prioritizes or exits consumer discrete GPU segment to maximize data-center margins. Consumer cards become niche/legacy. Gaming GPU market bifurcates from AI silicon. Street prices extreme or consumer tier disappears as a market.

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. Sourced from Bsky thread (proptermalone/lastnpcalex, 2026-04-22) — moat/VRAM debate. H1 (full reversion) = 35%: historical pattern of GPU price normalization after demand shocks (crypto GPU crunch 2021-2022 normalized by 2023), HBM4 fab buildout expected 2026-2027, AMD competitive pressure. H2 (moderate plateau) = 30%: data-center demand may ease partially but AI inference growth sustains some premium. H3 (sustained high) = 22%: Alex's thesis — structural demand outpaces fab expansion; prior reduced vs. H1/H2 because historical precedent favors normalization. H4 (structural escalation/bifurcation) = 13%: NVIDIA consumer exit is possible but faces antitrust and brand risk; treated as tail. No fired indicators at creation. Priors reflect base rates from prior GPU price cycles plus AI demand novelty discount.

Structural HBM supply constraint projected through 2028-2029 per SemiAnalysis. Hyperscaler memory capex 4x since 2023 indicates sustained demand pressure. No pre-committed indicator fires (t1_hbm_supply_normalization requires consumer allocation RESTORED, not constrained). Directionally supports H3/H4. Likelihoods weight H3 highest given projected supply gap timing aligns with 2028 resolution.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

Not recycled_intel vs ev_003: ev_003 was Q1 2026 data with higher spreads (5090 at 75-200%); this is April 2026 snapshot showing partial softening on 5080 but persistent premium. H2 weighted highest since current 5080 price sits squarely in H2 band with 2.5yr runway.

tier1_critical.desc: SK Hynix, Micron, and/or Samsung collectively announce HBM4 production volume sufficient for both data-center and consumer demand without consumer allocation cuts (confirmed in earnings or IDC memory report). Observable threshold: IDC or TechInsights reports consumer GPU HBM allocation restored to ≥2022 levels.

NVIDIA formally announces discontinuation or indefinite delay of consumer discrete GPU line (GeForce/RTX), dedicating all capacity to H-series/B-series data-center successors. Observable via official product roadmap announcement or 10-K filing.

tier2_strong.desc: 90-day average street price of RTX 5080-class (or current-gen equivalent) GPU within 15% of MSRP for ≥3 consecutive months, per TechPowerUp or NowInStock tracking.
AMD captures ≥25% of discrete GPU unit sales for ≥6 consecutive months per JonPeddie Research quarterly GPU report (increases competitive supply pressure).
≥2 major hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta) announce ≥20% GPU capex reduction in consecutive quarterly earnings calls — eases data-center demand pressure.

90-day average street price of RTX 5080-class (or current-gen equivalent) consumer GPU sits 50-100% above inflation-adjusted Q4-2024 baseline for ge 2 consecutive quarters across ge 3 major price trackers (TechPowerUp, NowInStock, TrackaLacker, BestValueGPU, Tom's Hardware GPU tracker).

tier3_suggestive.desc: TSMC announces ≥30% YoY capacity increase for 3nm/2nm process node (increases overall wafer supply that feeds both GPU and HBM production).

Published research (peer-reviewed or major lab technical report) demonstrates >50% reduction in per-inference VRAM requirements for frontier-equivalent models — reduces per-query GPU demand and enterprise VRAM pressure.

Median street price of top-tier consumer SKU (RTX 5090-class) exceeds 100% above inflation-adjusted Q4-2024 baseline for ge 2 consecutive quarters AND halo/prototype variants (e.g. 128GB cards) sustain >$10,000 pricing per Tom's Hardware / WccFTech tracking.

anti_indicators.desc: Consumer GPU waitlists >4 weeks at MSRP persist into 2028-Q2 per NowInStock tracking — demand structurally exceeds supply, full reversion implausible.
RTX 5090-class (or equivalent top-tier consumer GPU) street price exceeds 200% of MSRP for ≥1 month — moderate plateau trajectory no longer credible.
RTX 5080-class GPU street price within 10% of MSRP for ≥6 consecutive months per TechPowerUp — sustained high elevation thesis falsified.
NVIDIA launches new consumer-specific GPU series with ≥xx70-class at ≤$800 MSRP before 2028 — confirms continued commitment to consumer discrete GPU market.

Consumer GPU street prices remain >40% above inflation-adjusted Q4-2024 baseline through end of 2026-Q4 across major SKUs and trackers. Earlier-firing companion to anti_h1_persistent_waitlist (which requires 2028-Q2 persistence).

actorModel.description: Consumer VRAM pricing driven by NVIDIA supply strategy, AMD competitive pressure, HBM memory producers, and hyperscaler demand-side decisions.

nvidia.name: NVIDIA
nvidia.role: Supply-side monopolist — controls discrete GPU design and allocates HBM between consumer and data-center
nvidia.decisionStyle: Rational profit maximizer. Data-center gross margin (~70%) > consumer (~50%). Incentive to prioritize enterprise allocation.
biases: Short-term revenue maximization over long-term consumer brand loyalty
filters: Antitrust scrutiny if consumer GPU market is abandoned
overrides: If AMD gains >30% market share, NVIDIA may rebalance consumer allocation to defend share

amd.name: AMD (Radeon)
amd.role: Competitive challenger — benefits from NVIDIA supply constraints, incentivized to fill consumer gap
amd.decisionStyle: Opportunistic. RDNA architecture competitive at mid-range but trails NVIDIA at top tier.
biases: Historically slow to capture top-tier GPU segment despite competitive pricing
filters: TSMC capacity allocation constraints (shared with NVIDIA)
overrides: NVIDIA consumer exit would create a clear lane for AMD to capture enthusiast segment

hbm_producers.name: SK Hynix / Micron / Samsung
hbm_producers.role: Supply-side HBM memory oligopoly — allocation decisions between enterprise and consumer drive VRAM scarcity
hbm_producers.decisionStyle: Institutional/profit-driven. Enterprise HBM (H100/B200) at 5-8x premium over consumer GDDR. Rational to prioritize enterprise.
biases: Reluctance to invest in consumer HBM capacity when enterprise margins are higher
filters: Multi-year supply agreements with NVIDIA for data-center HBM
overrides: Consumer demand spike or government fab subsidies (CHIPS Act) could shift allocation

hyperscalers.name: Microsoft / Google / Meta / Amazon
hyperscalers.role: Demand-side — training and inference GPU capex directly competes with consumer GPU supply
hyperscalers.decisionStyle: Institutional/competitive. AI infrastructure seen as strategic moat; capex driven by competitive pressure rather than ROI.
biases: GPU hoarding behavior — buy ahead of demand to avoid supply constraints
filters: Quarterly earnings pressure if capex exceeds return thresholds
overrides: Macro downturn or AI investment write-downs could trigger rapid capex pullback

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — pricing trackers and earnings capex figures, not analyst predictions
WATCH HBM ALLOCATION — the single most important supply-side signal is memory producer allocation between enterprise and consumer
TRACK AMD SHARE — JonPeddie Research quarterly GPU shipments are the cleanest competitive signal
HYPERSCALER CAPEX IS UPSTREAM — Microsoft/Google/Meta/Amazon GPU capex guidance moves before consumer prices react
